title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Recall our last class
We created a market for extra credit.

The outcome was efficient—we exhausted all gains from trade.


But was it fair?

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
A question of endowments
Some of you happened to be given an extra credit point.

Some of you didn’t.


Even though the market outcome maximized total surplus, who benefited depended entirely on those initial endowments.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The fundamental distinction

Efficiency asks: How big is the pie?



Equity asks: How is the pie divided?



Failure to separate these is probably one of the biggest conceptual mistakes in policy today.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Today’s roadmap

Trends in inequality
What “fairness” means
Ideal redistribution (and why it’s impossible)
The costs of real-world redistribution
When distortions are actually good

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Efficiency

Whether the economy is set up to generate the maximum total benefits


Efficiency is about the size of the pie.


An efficient allocation leaves no gains from trade unexploited.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Equity

Whether the distribution of benefits is judged to be fair


Equity is about how the pie is divided.


Note: This is a normative judgment—outside economics per se.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Distortions

When policy drives a wedge between social value and private return


Distortions cause people to make decisions that don’t maximize total surplus.


They’re usually the cost of achieving a more equitable distribution.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Deadweight loss

The loss in total benefits that results from distortions


When we redistribute, the pie often shrinks.


Deadweight loss measures how much it shrinks.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Second best

The lowest-cost way to achieve a particular distribution


If we can’t achieve equity for free, what’s the most efficient way to get there?

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
US income inequality: The headline


Source: World Inequality Database. Shows share of national income going to top 10% vs bottom 50%.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
What happened?
From ~1980 onward:

Top 10% share rose from ~35% to ~45%
Bottom 50% share fell from ~20% to ~14%


The gains from economic growth went disproportionately to the top.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Another perspective: Top 1%
The top 1% now earns about 20% of all income.

In the 1970s, it was closer to 10%.



The very top has pulled away even from the rest of the top 10%.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Wealth inequality is even starker
Income: What you earn each year
Wealth: What you own (assets minus debts)

The top 1% owns roughly 35% of all wealth.


The bottom 50% owns about 1%.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
A global perspective


Data from World Inequality Report (2026), Chapter 2, Fig. 2.2 “Global economic weight is shifting back towards Asia”, page 55. Shows global income distribution by region over time.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The elephant curve
Over the past 40 years, who gained the most globally?


The global middle class (especially China, India)
The global top 1%



Who gained the least?



The working and middle classes in rich countries.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
How people view income inequality
Hvidberg et al (2023) asked Danish people about their views on income inequality


How unfair is income inequality overall?
How unfair is income inequality within different groups (e.g. same education group, gender, co-workers)?

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Their questions


Reference: Hvidgberg, Kreiner, and Stantcheva (2023). “Social Positions and Fairness Views on Inequality.” Review of Economic Studies.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Views of fairness differ



Finding: Inequality within co-worker and education groups more unfair than overall (i.e. ‘cohort’) inequality.


Reference: Hvidgberg, Kreiner, and Stantcheva (2023). “Social Positions and Fairness Views on Inequality.” Review of Economic Studies.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Why does inequality matter?
Inequality affects:

Political stability
Social mobility
Health outcomes
Trust in institutions


But whether it’s a problem depends on your values.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
section: Let’s find out what you think
text:
Let’s find out what you think
I’m going to describe some scenarios.

For each one, tell me what the fair outcome is.


[Poll: pollev.com/e4e]

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Scenario 1: The shipwreck
You’re on a lifeboat with 10 others. You have 100 units of food.

Option A: Everyone gets 10 units
Option B: The person who can ration best distributes it (total consumed: 120 equivalent units due to efficiency)
Option C: Whoever grabbed it first keeps it


What’s fair?

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Scenario 2: The inheritance
A wealthy person dies. Should their children:

A: Inherit everything (property rights)
B: Inherit nothing—it goes to charity (equal opportunity)
C: Inherit some, with the rest taxed (compromise)


What’s fair?

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Scenario 3: The kidney
One kidney is available. Three patients need it:

A: A 25-year-old who will live 40 more years
B: A 65-year-old who invented a life-saving drug
C: Whoever has waited longest on the list


What’s fair?

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The point
There’s no consensus on what “fair” means.

Different frameworks give different answers.


Let’s look at the main ones.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Utilitarianism

Maximize total well-being across all people.


Implication: Transfer resources until marginal utility is equalized.


If a dollar means more to a poor person than a rich person, transfer it.


Problem: Doesn’t care about distribution per se—only totals.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Rawlsian maximin

Maximize the well-being of the worst-off person.


John Rawls asked: What rules would you choose behind a “veil of ignorance”?


If you didn’t know your place in society, you’d protect the bottom.


Problem: Ignores everyone except the very worst off.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Libertarianism

Respect property rights and voluntary exchange.


Whatever distribution emerges from fair processes is fair.


Problem: Ignores how initial endowments were acquired.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Equality of opportunity

Everyone should have the same starting point.


Inequality of outcomes is okay if it results from fair competition.


Problem: Hard to define and achieve equal starting points.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Equality of outcomes

Everyone should end up with the same amount.


Problem: Eliminates incentives; practically impossible.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Where do you stand?
[Poll: Which framework resonates most with you?]


Utilitarian (maximize total well-being)
Rawlsian (protect the worst off)
Libertarian (respect property rights)
Equal opportunity
Equal outcomes

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The economist’s role
Economics can’t tell you which framework is “right.”

That’s a normative judgment—a question of values.


But economics can tell you:

What are the costs of achieving different distributions?

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
A remarkable result
Suppose the market allocation differs from your preferred “fair” outcome.

Can we get to the fair outcome without losing efficiency?


Yes—in theory.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The Second Welfare Theorem

Any efficient allocation can be achieved by:

Redistributing endowments with taxes and transfers
Then letting the market work



If we could just reshuffle who starts with what, markets would do the rest.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Back to our classroom market
Imagine I wanted to help students who most need the extra credit.

Ideal solution: Give the initial extra credit points to them instead.


Then let the market play out normally.


Efficient and equitable!

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The catch
This requires lump-sum taxes and transfers.

Lump-sum means:

Pre-specified amounts
Do not depend on your behavior or choices



Like a fixed tax you pay regardless of what you do.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Why lump-sum works
If a tax doesn’t depend on your choices, it doesn’t distort them.

You face the same incentives as before—you just start with less money.



No deadweight loss. No distortion. Just redistribution.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The problem
What could we actually tax in a lump-sum way?

We’d need to tax something immutable—that you can’t change.


One serious proposal: tax height.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Wait, really?
Mankiw & Weinzierl (2010) showed:

Taller people earn more on average
Height is (mostly) genetically determined
A height tax would be non-distortionary


Nobody actually proposes this. But it illustrates the problem.

Reference: Mankiw & Weinzierl (2010). “The Optimal Taxation of Height.” American Economic Journal: Economic Policy.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Back to Feeding America
Recall Feeding America’s market-based system for distributing food.

A key feature was the amount of fake money each food bank got to bid on food.


Importantly, we can

observe need (i.e. the number of poor people in a food bank’s area)
redistribute money to the needy



Second Welfare Theorem applies and the market finds the efficient allocation

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
section: Why we usually can’t do lump-sum
text:
Why we usually can’t do lump-sum
We can’t observe the things we’d need to observe:

Innate ability
Effort capacity
Future earnings potential


So we tax things we can observe: income, wealth, consumption.


But these depend on choices—so taxes distort behavior.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
A simple idea
Rich people have a lot of money.

Poor people need money.


Why not just tax the rich and give to the poor?

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The behavioral response
We covered this in our lecture on incentives.

When you tax income, you change the return to working.


Higher taxes → work becomes less attractive → people work less.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Recall the Laffer curve

At some point, raising rates actually lowers revenue.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The real cost
Even before we hit the peak, higher taxes cause:

Reduced labor supply
Less entrepreneurship
More tax avoidance


The tax base shrinks—so the pie gets smaller.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The tradeoff

We can make the distribution more equal…
…but often at the cost of a smaller pie.


This is the fundamental equity-efficiency tradeoff.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
How big is this cost?
Remember the elasticity of taxable income?

Best estimates suggest it’s around 0.25.


Not zero—but not enormous either.



We can redistribute, but there’s a cost.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
An illustration with our workers
Let’s go back to our construction workers and content creators.

Suppose society decides it’s unfair that TikTokers might earn more than construction workers.


Policy: Tax TikTok earnings and subsidize construction wages.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
What happens?
The tax drives a wedge between what consumers pay and what creators receive.

Some content creators switch to construction work.


But wait—these are people whose comparative advantage is in TikToks!

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text: The distortion visualized

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The math
Before the tax: \[P_H \cdot H_0 + P_T \cdot T_0 = \text{Total Value}\]

After the tax, people reallocate. But: \[P_H \cdot H_1 + P_T \cdot T_1 < P_H \cdot H_0 + P_T \cdot T_0\]



Total value produced has fallen—even though we collected tax revenue.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Why the loss?
We’ve shifted production to people who are relatively bad at it.

Content creators forced into construction produce fewer houses than real construction workers would.


Construction workers staying put don’t make up the difference.



Deadweight loss: The economy produces less than it could.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
section: What if we used lump-sum transfers?
text:
What if we used lump-sum transfers?
Suppose we could tax content creators a fixed amount—regardless of whether they work in TikTok or construction.

Then the tax doesn’t affect their work decisions.


No reallocation. No distortion. No deadweight loss.


But we can’t observe “being a content creator” independent of their choices.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Recall externalities
An externality is when your action affects others who aren’t part of the transaction.


Negative: Pollution, congestion, secondhand smoke
Positive: Vaccination, education

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The problem with externalities
Without intervention, markets produce:

Too much of goods with negative externalities
Too little of goods with positive externalities


Prices don’t reflect the true social costs and benefits.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The Pigouvian solution
Arthur Pigou’s insight: Tax negative externalities, subsidize positive ones.

A tax equal to the external cost makes people internalize the harm.



A distortion that corrects a market failure can increase efficiency.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Carbon tax example
Burning fossil fuels causes climate change.

A carbon tax makes polluters pay for this harm.


Result: Less pollution, and higher total surplus.



This is a “good” distortion—it fixes a broken price signal.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The key insight
Distortions are costly when they push us away from efficiency.

But when markets are already failing, the right distortion can push us toward efficiency.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Public goods
Some goods can’t be provided by markets:

National defense
Basic research
Public health infrastructure


These require tax revenue to provide.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The necessity of taxes
If we need taxes to fund public goods, we’ll have some distortion.

The goal isn’t to eliminate distortion—it’s to minimize it.



Raise the revenue you need in the least costly way possible.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Efficient taxation
Economic research suggests:

Broad bases, low rates minimize distortion
Tax things that are hard to change (land, consumption)
Avoid taxing things that are easy to move (capital, mobile workers)


This is why economists often prefer consumption taxes to income taxes.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Sometimes we can do both
In rare cases, we can increase both efficiency and equity:


Removing regulations that protect the wealthy (occupational licensing, zoning)
Fixing market failures that hurt the poor (predatory lending, pollution in poor neighborhoods)
Investing in human capital (education for disadvantaged kids)



These are win-wins—but they’re not always available.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
The hard cases
Most of the time, we face real tradeoffs.

More redistribution → more distortion → smaller pie.


The question isn’t whether to redistribute—it’s how much.



The answer depends on your values and the costs.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Okun’s Leaky Bucket Analogy
Imagine you want to move water from point A to point B.

The only catch is that your bucket has holes in it.


Ideally, you’d patch the holes, or use a different bucket.


But most of the time we can’t do that. Water leaks out and is lost.


This analogy illustrates the key point: redistribution can be costly.


How much leakage do you accept?

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
section: What actually is the leakage? An example
text:
What actually is the leakage? An example
Sometimes it’s monetary, but can be broader than that.

In 1696, King William II levied the ‘window tax’, which charged people based on how many windows they had.


Intended as a progressive tax:

Poorer people lived in houses with fewer windows.
Fewer windows meant a lower tax bill.


Reference: Oates & Schwab (2015). “The Window Tax: A Case Study in Excess Burden.” Journal of Economic Perspectives.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
section: What actually is the leakage? An example
text:
What actually is the leakage? An example
The tax was a public health nightmare


People bricked up their windows to avoid the tax.
This reduced light and ventilation in homes.
Public health suffered as a result.



This is the leakage or deadweight loss economists talk about.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Key takeaways

Efficiency and equity are distinct—don’t conflate size with distribution



Fairness is normative—economics can’t tell you what’s fair




Ideal redistribution is impossible—lump-sum taxes aren’t feasible




Real redistribution has costs—distortions reduce total output




Sometimes distortions help—when correcting externalities

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Thinking like an economist
An economist holds both ideas in mind:


Markets are powerful tools for efficiency
Distribution matters and may require intervention




The goal is to understand the tradeoffs and quantify them.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text:
Next time
We’ll explore how economists actually measure the effects of policies.

How do we know if a policy worked?


The answer: Causation and experiments.

title: Equity vs. Efficiency
text: Questions?

title: Lectures
text:
Lecture slides will be posted here as the course progresses.

Incentives
Markets and Their Failures
Equity vs. Efficiency
Uncertainty
Quasi-Experiments
Risk and Insurance

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Why do we need quasi-experiments?
Many important questions can’t be answered with randomized experiments:


Does the minimum wage reduce employment?
Does going to prison increase or decrease future crime?
Does expanding health insurance improve health outcomes?




The problem: We can’t randomly assign wages, prison sentences, or health coverage.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Natural experiments
Sometimes the world gives us variation that looks like an experiment.

Natural experiment: Real-world variation that allows researchers to identify causal effects without explicit randomization.


The key insight: Find settings where treatment is determined by factors unrelated to outcomes.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The quasi-experimental toolkit
Today we’ll cover four major approaches:


Interrupted time series — Before vs. after a sudden change




Difference-in-differences — Compare changes across groups




Regression discontinuity — Exploit arbitrary cutoffs




Instrumental variables — Use external sources of variation

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
What’s the catch?
There’s no free lunch.

Each method requires identifying assumptions — conditions that must hold for the estimates to be causal.


And we need specification tests — diagnostics to assess whether those assumptions are plausible.



Key insight: Being a good economist means knowing both the methods and their limitations.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The minimum wage debate
Textbook economics: Higher minimum wage → Less employment

The argument:

Higher wages = higher labor costs
Firms hire fewer workers
Some workers lose their jobs



But is this what actually happens?

title: Quasi-Experiments
section: The challenge of studying minimum wage
text:
The challenge of studying minimum wage
Why can’t we just compare states with different minimum wages?

Selection problem: States that raise minimum wages might be different in many ways.

Stronger economies
Different industries
Different political preferences



Any employment difference could reflect these factors, not the minimum wage.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Card and Krueger’s natural experiment
In 1992, New Jersey raised its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05.

Neighboring Pennsylvania did not change its minimum wage.




New Jersey

Treatment group
Minimum wage: $4.25 → $5.05


Pennsylvania

Control group
Minimum wage: $4.25 (unchanged)

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The study design
Card and Krueger surveyed fast food restaurants in both states:


Before: February 1992 (before NJ increase)
After: November 1992 (after NJ increase)



They measured employment at each restaurant.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: What did they find?

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The surprising result
Employment in New Jersey increased relative to Pennsylvania.

This challenged the conventional wisdom that minimum wage increases destroy jobs.



The lesson: Economic theory gives us predictions, but data tells us what actually happens.

title: Quasi-Experiments
section: Why might the textbook be wrong?
text:
Why might the textbook be wrong?
Several explanations have been proposed:


Monopsony power — Employers have wage-setting power




Efficiency wages — Higher wages increase productivity




Reduced turnover — Less costly hiring and training




Price adjustments — Costs passed to consumers

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Discussion: What makes this convincing?
Think about:

Why is Pennsylvania a good comparison for New Jersey?
What could still go wrong with this design?
Would you be convinced?

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The simplest quasi-experiment
Idea: Compare outcomes before and after a sudden change.

title: Quasi-Experiments
section: Example: Police violence and civic engagement
text:
Example: Police violence and civic engagement
Ang et al. (2025) studied how people’s engagement with police changed after George Floyd’s murder in May 2020.

The event: A dramatic, sudden shift in public attention to police violence.


The question: Did this attention reduce engagement with, and favorability of, police?

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: What the data showed

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: What the data showed

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Interpreting the results
Police favorability and crime reporting rates fell significantly after the event.

But is this causal? What else changed in May 2020?


Potential confounds:

COVID-19 pandemic
Protests and civil unrest
Policy changes in many departments
Economic disruption

title: Quasi-Experiments
section: The limits of interrupted time series
text:
The limits of interrupted time series


Strengths:

Simple and intuitive
Works with just one group
Easy to visualize


Weaknesses:

Can’t separate the event from other changes
Assumes trend would have continued
No control group to compare against




Bottom line: Good for description, risky for causal claims.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The key insight
What if we could account for time trends using a comparison group?

Difference-in-differences (DiD):

Compare treated group before and after (first difference)
Compare control group before and after (second difference)
Subtract to remove common trends (difference in differences)

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: The DiD logic

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The parallel trends assumption
For DiD to work, we need to assume:


Without treatment, the treated and control groups would have followed parallel trends.



This is the key identifying assumption.


We can never prove it’s true (we don’t observe the counterfactual), but we can look for evidence against it.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Testing parallel trends
Pre-trends test: Do the groups have similar trends before treatment?

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Example: Medicaid expansion and mortality
Sommers et al. (2012) studied how Medicaid coverage affects mortality.

Some states expanded between 2000 and 2002, others did not.


Research question: Did Medicaid expansion reduce deaths?

Sommers et al. (2012). “Mortality and Access to Care among Adults after State Medicaid Expansions.” The New England Journal of Medicine.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The study design


Treatment:
States that expanded Medicaid

Control:
Neighboring states that did not expand


Compare changes in mortality rates before and after expansion.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: DiD in the wild

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Results: Lives saved
The study found that Medicaid expansion reduced mortality.

Approximately 1 death prevented per 176 additional people covered.


Effects were largest for:

People aged 35-64
Poorer counties
Minorities


Sommers et al. (2012). “Mortality and Access to Care among Adults after State Medicaid Expansions.” The New England Journal of Medicine.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Example: Cancer clusters and housing
Davis (2004) studied what happens to housing prices when people learn about possible health risks

The event: Higher than expected leukemia rates in Churchill County, Nevada, during the late 90s


Treatment: People living in Churchill County
Control: People living in nearby county (Lyon County)

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Davis (2004) DiD


Reference: Davis (2004). “The Effect of Health Risk on Housing Values: Evidence from a Cancer Cluster.” American Economic Review.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
What they found



Reference: Davis (2004). “The Effect of Health Risk on Housing Values: Evidence from a Cancer Cluster.” American Economic Review.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Discussion: When does DiD work?
Think about:

What would make you believe the parallel trends assumption?
When might treated and control groups trend differently?
How would you choose a control group?

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The intuition
Many programs enforce sharp cutoffs:

You’re eligible if your score is above X
You get the scholarship if your income is below Y
You’re assigned to the advanced class if you score above Z


People just above and just below the cutoff are nearly identical…


…except one group gets treated and the other doesn’t.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: The RD design

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Why this works
Key assumption: Around the cutoff, treatment is essentially random

For example, someone who scored 79 vs 81 on a test:

Very similar abilities
But one gets the program, one doesn’t




The discontinuity creates a mini-experiment.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Example: Class size and learning
Angrist and Lavy (1999) studied how class size affects student achievement.

The setting: Israel has a rule (Maimonides’ Rule) that caps classes at 40 students.



40 students → 1 class of 40
41 students → 2 classes of ~20

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: The class size discontinuity

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Results (reading comprehension)



Implication: Smaller classes improved scores.


Angrist and Lavy (1999). “Using Maimonides’ Rule to Estimate the Effect of Class Size on Scholastic Achievement.” Quarterly Journal of Economics.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Example: The electoral incumbent effect
Lee (2008) studied whether winning an election affects future electoral success.

The cutoff: 50% of the vote

49.9% → You lose
50.1% → You win



Candidates just above and below 50% had essentially identical support.


Does winning now generate future electoral success?

title: Quasi-Experiments
section: Barely win today, more likely to win again
text: Barely win today, more likely to win again

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
What winning gets you
Incumbents are about 45 percentage points more likely to win the next election


Access to campaign resources
Name recognition
Ability to deliver for constituents


Lee (2008). “Randomized Experiments from Non-random Selection in U.S. House Elections.” Journal of Econometrics.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Example: Returns to public universities
Mountjoy (2026) studied the effects of attending a four-year public university.

Admissions cutoffs means many students are at the margin of admission.

Just above the SAT/ACT cutoff → Four-year college
Just below → Two-year college

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Higher earnings later on


Mountjoy (2026). “Marginal Returns to Public Universities.” Quarterly Journal of Economics.

title: Quasi-Experiments
section: What can go wrong with RD?
text:
What can go wrong with RD?
Manipulation: What if people can game the cutoff?


Students retaking tests until they pass
Doctors rounding blood pressure readings
Officials adjusting income reports



Test: Check for bunching at the cutoff.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: Testing for manipulation

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: Example: Mountjoy (2026)

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Discussion: Finding discontinuities
Think about:

What policies create sharp cutoffs?
Where might you look for RD opportunities?
What makes a cutoff credible?

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The problem
What if treatment is always correlated with outcomes?

Example: Does education increase earnings?

People who get more education might be:

More motivated
More able
From wealthier families




We can’t just compare educated vs. less-educated people.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The IV solution
Find something that:

Affects whether you get treated (education)
Only affects the outcome (earnings) through treatment


This is called an instrumental variable or instrument.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: The IV logic

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Example: Birthday and education
Angrist and Krueger (1991) used quarter of birth as an instrument for education.

Why quarter of birth matters:

School entry cutoffs are based on birthdate
Compulsory schooling laws require attendance until age 16
People born earlier in the year can drop out with less education

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Their argument
Someone born in January vs. December:


Essentially random (parents don’t plan this)
But affects years of schooling due to compulsory schooling requirements
Quarter of birth shouldn’t directly affect earnings



So any earnings difference must be through education!

Angrist and Krueger (1991). “Does Compulsory School Attendance Affect Schooling and Earnings?” Quarterly Journal of Economics.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: First stage: Birthday to education

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: Second stage: Education to earnings

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Conclusions
Using quarter of birth as an instrument:

Each additional year of schooling increases earnings by about 10%.


This is similar to (but more credibly causal than) simple comparisons.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Example: Eviction and poverty
Collinson et al. (2024) studied the consequences of eviction.

The problem: People who get evicted are different:

Lower income
Less stable employment
May have other challenges



The instrument: Random assignment of cases to judges who differ in harshness.

Collinson, Humphries, Mader, Reed, Tannenbaum, and van Dijk (2024). “Eviction and Poverty in American Cities.” Quarterly Journal of Economics.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Judge leniency as an instrument
Some judges are “tough” (evict more often), some are “lenient.”


Cases are randomly assigned to judges
Your judge’s leniency predicts whether you’re evicted
But judge assignment shouldn’t directly affect your outcomes



This isolates the causal effect of eviction itself.

title: Quasi-Experiments
section: Stricter judge, higher chance of eviction …
text: Stricter judge, higher chance of eviction …

title: Quasi-Experiments
text: … more likely to become homeless

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Conclusions
Being evicted (due to a harsh judge) leads to:


Homelessness: More frequent shelter use
Earnings: Lower earnings
Financial distress: Worse credit score and overall financial health


Collinson et al. (2024). “Eviction and Poverty in American Cities.” Quarterly Journal of Economics.

title: Quasi-Experiments
section: What can go wrong with IV?
text:
What can go wrong with IV?
Problem 1: Weak instruments
If the instrument barely affects treatment, estimates are noisy and biased.

Problem 2: Exclusion restriction violations
What if the instrument affects outcomes directly?

Quarter of birth → seasonal differences in health?
Judge harshness → affects other case characteristics?

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The exclusion restriction

The instrument must affect the outcome only through the treatment.


This assumption can never be tested directly.


We rely on:

Economic reasoning
Checking for obvious violations
Testing robustness to different instruments

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Matching methods to questions



Method
When to use
Key assumption




ITS
Sudden, universal change
No other changes


DiD
Treatment varies across groups
Parallel trends


RD
Sharp eligibility cutoff
No manipulation


IV
External source of variation
Exclusion restriction

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The hierarchy of evidence


Randomized experiments — Gold standard, but often infeasible




Natural experiments — Next best, requires careful validation




Observational comparisons — Last resort, highly susceptible to bias

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
What makes a study convincing?

Transparency: Authors show their work, including failures


Robustness: Results hold under different specifications


Mechanisms: Findings align with economic theory


Replication: Other researchers find similar results

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The skeptic’s checklist
When reading a quasi-experimental study, ask:


What is the identifying assumption?
What specification tests do they run?
What could violate the assumption?
How robust are the results?
Does the mechanism make sense?

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Discussion: Designing your own study
Imagine you want to know:

Does remote work increase or decrease productivity?


How would you design a study using each method?

Interrupted time series?
Difference-in-differences?
Regression discontinuity?
Instrumental variables?

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
What we learned today

Quasi-experiments let us find causal effects without randomization.


Each method has identifying assumptions that must be carefully evaluated.


Specification tests help us assess whether assumptions are plausible.


Being a good economist means knowing both methods and their limitations.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
The power of quasi-experiments
These methods have transformed economics:


Minimum wage research changed policy debates
Medicaid studies informed healthcare policy
Education research shaped school funding decisions
Criminal justice research influenced sentencing reform




You can change the world by finding the right natural experiment.

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Next time
We’ll continue with labor markets and the future of work:

How technology changes labor demand
The economics of artificial intelligence
What skills will matter in the future

title: Quasi-Experiments
text:
Further reading
For those interested in learning more:

Angrist & Pischke, Mastering ’Metrics (accessible introduction)
Card, “The Elusive Search for Negative Wage Effects” (minimum wage review)
Lee & Lemieux, “Regression Discontinuity Designs in Economics” (RD methods)

title: Uncertainty
text:
Switching gears
You’ve learned one of the most important lessons about data analysis:

Correlation does not imply causation.


Today we dive into a second crucial issue:

How to think about uncertainty.

title: Uncertainty
text:
A motivating example
Imagine we run an experiment in Chicago Heights.


Randomly assign students to earn incentive payments for higher test scores
Treatment group scores 11% higher than control



Great! But we run the experiment again…



This time the effect is 15%



What is the true effect? 11%? 15%? Something else?

title: Uncertainty
text:
The fundamental insight
Even under ideal conditions—a carefully designed randomized experiment—

Data do not give us the truth. They give us an estimate.


An estimate is hopefully informative about the truth, but we need to ask: how informative?

title: Uncertainty
text:
You’ve seen this before
“Margin of error” on a political poll

“Confidence interval” on an effect estimate


“Statistically significant” effect of a new drug


These are all ways of quantifying uncertainty.

title: Uncertainty
text:
Key ideas for today

Parameters vs. estimates: The difference between the “true” effect and what data give us


Statistical uncertainty: How informative is any estimate about the underlying truth?


Sampling uncertainty: Uncertainty from only seeing a portion of the full data


Confidence intervals: Measures of which parameters are consistent with your data


Bayesian reasoning: Combining evidence with prior knowledge

title: Uncertainty
text:
A suspicious evening
You’re at the craps table. The dice keep coming up snake eyes way more than you’d like.

Is the casino cheating?


When nobody’s looking, you steal one of the dice to investigate later.
(You’re still many hundreds in the hole.)

title: Uncertainty
text:
Quick probability check
If the die is fair, what’s the chance it comes up 1?


1 in 6 (about 16.7%)



If we roll twice, what’s the chance of getting 1 both times?



1/6 × 1/6 = 1/36 (about 2.8%)



How many 1s in a row before you get suspicious?

title: Uncertainty
text:
A more formal test
The casino probably isn’t that clumsy. You decide to run a proper experiment:

Roll the die 100 times and count how often it comes up 1.


If the die is fair, how many 1s do you expect?

title: Uncertainty
text:
What do you think?
[Poll Everywhere: You roll a fair die 100 times. How many 1s would make you suspicious?]

title: Uncertainty
text:
What does the math say?

Even a fair die won’t give you exactly 16-17 ones every time.

title: Uncertainty
text:
The key insight

Even if the die is fair, there’s randomness affecting each roll.


We can’t expect exactly 1/6 ones. But we can quantify the range of outcomes we might see.


If we get 20 ones out of 100, is that suspicious?


If we get 30 ones? 40 ones?


How can we reduce our uncertainty?

title: Uncertainty
text: More data helps

title: Uncertainty
text:
Why does more data help?
As we collect more data, we average out the randomness.

With 10 rolls, getting 3 ones (30%) isn’t unusual.


With 1,000 rolls, getting 300 ones (30%) would be extremely suspicious.



More data = Less uncertainty

title: Uncertainty
text:
Same idea, different context
The die example is a type of sampling uncertainty.


We want to know: How often would this die show 1 if rolled infinitely many times?
We observe: The results of 100 rolls (a sample)



This same logic applies whenever our data is just a sample from a larger population.

title: Uncertainty
text:
Polling example
You want to measure the share of voters who will support Republicans in the midterms.

Ideal approach: Ask every voter in America.


Reality: That’s prohibitively expensive. So you ask a random sample.

title: Uncertainty
text:
How useful is your sample?
If you ask 1 voter:
How confident are you that they represent all voters?

If you ask 10 voters:
A bit better, but still pretty uncertain.


If you ask 1,000 voters:
Much more confident that your estimate is close to the truth.

title: Uncertainty
text:
The question
You poll 100 voters and find 48% support Republicans.

Your goal is to know the true support in the full population.



How close is 48% to the truth?



Thankfully, there are tools to answer this question.

title: Uncertainty
section: The easiest way to think about it
text:
The easiest way to think about it
Suppose the true Republican vote share is 49%.

If we sample 100 voters at random, what estimates might we get?


We could simulate this thousands of times and see the distribution of possible results.

title: Uncertainty
text:
Building a confidence interval
You poll 100 voters and find 48% support Republicans.

Now ask: Which true values could have plausibly generated 48%?


For each possible true value, we can simulate what estimates we’d get…

title: Uncertainty
text:
Testing the truth: Is it 50%?
If the true share were 50%, simulations show we’d get estimates like 48% pretty often.


50% is consistent with our data.

title: Uncertainty
text:
Testing the truth: Is it 55%?
If the true share were 55%, getting 48% would be unusual but not crazy.


55% is borderline—maybe consistent, maybe not.

title: Uncertainty
text:
Testing the truth: Is it 70%?
If the true share were 70%, getting 48% would be extremely unlikely.


70% is inconsistent with our data. We can rule it out.

title: Uncertainty
text:
The confidence interval
A 95% confidence interval is the set of true values that could have generated our estimate at least 95% of the time.

title: Uncertainty
section: What does the length tell us?
text:
What does the length tell us?
The length of the interval reflects your precision.


Short CI = You can rule out many values (precise estimate)
Long CI = Many values are still plausible (imprecise estimate)



More data → shorter confidence intervals.

title: Uncertainty
text:
Hypothesis tests
Sometimes we just want a yes/no answer:

Are the data consistent with a particular value?


This is called a hypothesis test.


Most commonly: Can we rule out zero effect?


If zero is outside our confidence interval, we say the result is statistically significant.

title: Uncertainty
text:
An important caveat
Statistically significant ≠ Important


A tiny effect can be “significant” with enough data
A big effect can be “not significant” with limited data



Significance just means: unlikely to be pure noise

title: Uncertainty
text:
A subtle problem
Even our statistical tests are subject to uncertainty.

I can never tell you with certainty whether the true vote share is 53% or 51%.


There’s always some chance my estimate is just a fluke.

title: Uncertainty
text:
What happens with many tests?
Suppose I give you 1,000 dice.

You don’t know this, but all of them are actually fair.


You test each die by rolling it 10 times.


You decide that any die showing 5 or more ones is “suspicious”.

title: Uncertainty
text: The result

title: Uncertainty
text:
The problem
Getting 5+ ones out of 10 rolls happens about 2% of the time for a fair die.

But with 1,000 dice, you expect about 20 dice to “look suspicious.”


These aren’t loaded—they’re false positives.



More tests = More flukes

title: Uncertainty
text:
P-hacking
This is called the multiple hypothesis testing problem.

If I run many experiments and only report the winner, my tests become unreliable.


Cherry-picking results this way is sometimes called p-hacking.

title: Uncertainty
text:
Be skeptical

Thinking like an economist means being skeptical when someone shows a “significant” result that may just be the survivor of many trials.


Always ask:

How many things did they test?
Are they showing all results or just the “winners”?

title: Uncertainty
text:
Two approaches to uncertainty
Everything we’ve discussed is sometimes called frequentist reasoning:


Quantify uncertainty from seeing limited data—if we repeated the study, we might get different results.



There’s another approach: Bayesian reasoning.

title: Uncertainty
text:
Thomas Bayes (1701-1761)





Bayesian reasoning combines evidence with your prior knowledge.

Portrait from Wikipedia Commons. Bayes was an English statistician and Presbyterian minister.

title: Uncertainty
text:
Let’s try it
Thanks to the registrar, I know the average age of everyone in this room.

What’s your guess?

Poll Everywhere: What do you think the average age in this room is? (in years)

title: Uncertainty
text:
Now let’s collect some data

Pick 3-4 students at random, have them announce their ages. Compute the sample average live.


Do you want to change your guess?

Poll Everywhere: Updated guess for average age?

title: Uncertainty
text:
What just happened?
You started with prior beliefs about the average age.

You saw evidence (the sample of ages).


You updated to form posterior beliefs.



This is Bayesian reasoning.

title: Uncertainty
text:
The key questions

How much did your guess change?
What determines how much weight you put on the evidence vs. your prior?


Factors that matter:

How confident you were initially
How much evidence you saw
How surprising the evidence was

title: Uncertainty
text:
Uncertainty still matters
Whether you think like a frequentist or a Bayesian:

Being able to think about the precision of your estimates is essential.


Uncertainty determines how much weight to put on evidence vs. prior beliefs.

title: Uncertainty
text:
What we learned today
Data give us estimates, not truth.

Uncertainty is unavoidable—even in perfect experiments.


More data reduces uncertainty by averaging out randomness.


Confidence intervals tell us which parameter values are consistent with our data.


Multiple testing creates false positives—be skeptical of cherry-picked results.

title: Uncertainty
text:
The bottom line

Thinking like an economist means understanding that results are subject to statistical uncertainty—and knowing how to quantify it.


Whether you’re reading a poll, evaluating a drug trial, or running your own experiment, always ask:

How confident should I be?
